With  the same logic, wouldn't this shape be better for wind propellers near inhabited areas? I seem to recall MIT making quieter drones with this.

author: @pir869
text:
Why did you not use a normal dc motor or for that matter an AC generator , both of which rotate more freely than a stepper motor , i have yet to see a stepper motor used in mainstream power generation . As the maker of the toroid blade stated about the orientation of convex vs concave i don't see that doing anything other than change rotation direction , but i would be inclined to try different wind direction angles to see if this increases or worsens the outcome.

As for the design ,i've seen a few videos about this in boating applications with lower rpm giving the same thrust , and as water and air are both considered fluid mediums with different viscosities the same should be for air , with a reversal of air as a generator it should create more torque.

I would like to try it myself as i want to make a hand crank and wind powered generator for a super capacitor power bank to feed  AC inverters here in the UK (Scotland bit), i'm pretty sure pvc pipe could be cut into even strips and bonded to a pvc hub with the proper twist added to make each petal.

I do enjoy the video series and get some good ideas , i have a copied book on AC motors used as water turbine generators , i believe you wanted to know the hows and whys of the cap calculations etc , if you can pm me i'll send the pdf i have for this , maybe you could update the  turbine diy generator video ,likely a hit with the video audience.

Best wishes.

author: @HostileRespite
text: Toroidal props are just inverted Archimedes screws cut into short sections. Their advantage in water is negating cavitation. Under water cavitation is a destructive force due to microbursting air pockets which form when water is "cut" at high speeds. It's not unlike breaking the sound barrier in the air, only this happens at much lower speeds under more dense water. When this happens the microbursting air bubbles cause damage to the surface of the part over time. This is hardly noticeable to external propellers where the bursts can dissipate their shock to the surrounding water but do affect the noise and thrust... The microburst can also cause huge spikes in pressure for internal parts like impellers and can crack the engine casing entirely. So reducing underwater cavitation is a big deal. It's just disappointing that toroidal props only seem to offer a slight noise reduction for aerial applications and don't seem to offer any other real benefits... yet.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
seems like the angle of the blade is more important than it's shape, just as you found that a flat sheet at 35º was the sweet spot for redirecting oncoming wind downward into your tunnel for that project, and so the angle of the bladed needs to be more obtuse to the wind up to a point, to redirect it, (which is what generator blades are doing, the more redirection the more energy they can take from that incoming wind, while the propeller the more obtuse to it, the more energy it can impart into it as it redirects it, say for thrust.) 

what I want to know is can this angle be changed as the speed of the blade is moved to get more gains, or is this a fools errand and it changes nothing. :) 

the one benefit I can see these blades once the angle is set correctly if that bowed tip can reduce air current vibrations, would be on giant turbines they worry about birds and bats being harmed by the sonic vibrations created, but the direction you are going of many smaller units coupled together to get the higher value amount and in parallel so each one is redundancy, also completely does away with the worry to birds, bats and such because any sound they produce is so small it gets absorbed by air particles and reduced quickly so they don't have the range of influence on the environment, sonically that is. :)

author: @ericwazhung
text:
:/ Disappointed... 
Here's the guy who mixed highlighter-pen ink with gelatin and laughed with infectious glee when it questionably squeezed out a few extra milliwatts from a solar panel... Now we've a stepper motor as a generator as a reliable test? "Steppers are 80% efficient," he says... but this means nothing without a side-by-side and more detail... NEMA has to do with mounting, not winding-resistance, turns, or even the size/spacing/type/strength of the rotor's magnets. If you shine a spotlight on a tiny calculator solar cell mounted in an 8x10 picture frame, it won't give you much more power than a fluorescent ceiling lamp would. And nowhere near the power of an 8x10 solar panel with room lighting.
Not saying I expect a huge difference, nor even improvement, from these blades, but I am saying that you seem surprisingly/uncharacteristically opposed to giving them an honest chance. Almost like you're either afraid of, or hiding, something.

Here's an idea I'd've considered more characteristic of you: I wonder if a toroidal prop could be made of little more than a twisted strip of paper stapled to a hub. Its flexibility may actually be beneficial for different wind speeds and loads, a bit of a centripetal governer... More of a sail than a prop. And, most of all: easy to build. Which is right up your alley as someone usually trying to inspire folk to try things that aren't necessarily the most-efficient, but *doable*.
Maybe I missed a vid?

author: @Astro_Alphard
text:
I suspect the reason you're not getting anything out of those toroidal propellers is due to 2 reasons:
1.  the models on the internet are not designed for wind turbines and are instead designed as propellers. I personally recommend lowering the angle of attack (making the blade angle closer to the vertical axis by about 5 degrees) and using a slightly thicker airfoil at the root. 
2. You held it backwards the entire time, I can see the camber on the blades. It's like using a solar panel as an LED, possible but not very efficient! 

The advantages toroidal propellers have over their more traditional cousins is faster wake recovery, elimination of wingtip vortices, and a more evenly distributed turbulence area. This means that you can place wind turbines closer together for the same energy output. I don't think anyone is making toroidal propellers for wind turbines but if you do the aerodynamic characteristics of wind turbines and propellers are similar yet very different!

Looks a great 3D print but when I make these 'windmills' as garden ornaments I always orientate the concave edge of the blade into the wind. My guess is that it will catch more wind this way, very much like an anemometer cup. I noticed in your test that you had the convex side of the blade facing your wind source and maybe this lessened the efficiency of the blades. Anyway as I say, I make these windmills as garden ornaments and not for power generation, finding that they turn effortlessly in the slightest breeze and the toroidal blades are very strong compared to single blades. My 6 petal version, which I like to see as having effectively 12 blades spins even better and faster. Keep up the great work, love your videos...

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
I still don’t see how these tests are “proper”.  All your tests, so far, seemed like comparing apples and oranges. 
First a 2 bladed toroidal and a 3 bladed standard propeller, of equal diameter, with a common wind source. 
Next, a much larger diameter 4 bladed toroidal with an unknown (I couldn’t see it in the video) wind source. If the blower used in this test was the same one used in the first test then it would only be pushing a fraction of the toroidal blade surface at any one time.
To me, a “proper” test would be of a 3 bladed toroidal and 3 bladed standard propellers of equal diameters and a single blower used on both propellers.
I have no idea what the results will show but they should show if one blade is more efficient than the other.

author: @austinbambooinc2507
text: The very things that make it better as a cavitation free propeller keep it from catching enough wind to work as a turbine. Rather than increasing the blade length, it would work better if you increased the "depth" of the blades. It's too flat, so rather than properly diverting the wind and gaining momentum, it's just resisting it.

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: I'd like to see the efficient comparisons to a ducted propeller.  I suspect that most of the gains with toroidal props comes from them essentially having their own moving duct included instead of open blade tips.  Rather than the air sliding off the tips it is "scooped" and sent back in the same direction as the rest of the air.  I very well may be wrong but when I look at them and see them in motion it's what I see happening.  So any gains they'd have over a standard prop for a wind generator would likely come from lower velocity winds and even more so only with much larger prop blades.  Scale them up to sale 5 -6 metres and then I think you'll start seeing noticeable differences.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text:
Good demonstration!  I especially appreciate the use of the load meter for measurement of amps and volts.  
You are probably completely right in your conclusion, but your critics won't approve.  A toroidal "blade" has more than one aerodynamic surface in the swept area, with the same outer strength reinforcement as a ducted blade prop.  A four-blade toroidal propeller should best be compared to an eight-blade ducted fan prop.  Both are going to suffer from increased drag within a given swept area in a turbine drive configuration, and neither would be as effective as a three-bladed, simple prop in the same swept area and wind speed.  I don't think that a toroidal blade can be feathered, either, as they are in MW-scale turbines.  However, one proposition to answer to the problem of bird and bat killing by conventional turbines is using a ducted fan blade, and the toroidal design may answer some of the same issues without the same engineering complications.

author: @JoesWebPresence
text: No. You are thinking statically, and not at all like an aeronautical engineer. It's already a proven innovation in water propulsion. Those blades are supposed to give more lift at lower speeds and the entire performance curve slightly better than any comparative blade. Quieter too. To see if it's truly better, you need to compare it to a similar diameter blade at various speeds, on an identical shaft held better than just by hand. You need a dB tester too. Alternatively, clip them on to a performance drone and time it through a measured course, recording the noises they make. More fun, but given you are more interested in the generation possibilities, I'm sure something could be rigged up and hooked to that multimeter.

author: @nirodha7028
text: These are meant to ‘drive’ something at high rpm… and have advantages ‘then’… Not to be driven by wind at low rpm. Their positive effect lies in their ability to get rid of or greatly reduce the tip vortices which arise only at those high rpms. Those vortices consume energy which isn’t adding to forward motion… hence getting rid of them helps make propulsion more efficient. Essentially you are now pushing a car by hand with a 600 HP engine and measuring the ability of the dynamo to fill the battery… and compare that number to a 100 HP engines car being pushed at the same speed and stating the 600 HP engine doesn’t add any performance to the car. Lol… carry on 😂

author: @1992jamo
text: Thanks for the video. These things are only more efficient when the blades would otherwise cause cavitation. While you're not interested in them as propellers, that is where they shine (at much higher RPM).

replies.author: @helenlawson8426
replies.text:
This^
The Toroidal Prop was designed for reduced cavitation at high speed and in water. With a redesign & enough revs you might get it to work reasonably good as a flight propeller but as a blade for a wind turbine I can't see much point. Better to play with blade tip design on a normal blade to increase efficiency & reduce noise. Also just increasing the number of blades can reduce noise at the right speed. The right blade for the expected wind speed is very important.

author: @timothyneiswander3151
text:
There is a certain advantage to it looking cute.
Certain communities might oppose you putting up wind turbines because they don't like the look of them. Take an aesthetically pleasing blade design, give it a paint job to have an interesting visual effect in motion and you will get less opposition.

author: @C-M-E

It looks neat, therefor it must be better than standard.  SOP for the internet I'm afraid.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Without meaning offense it occurs to me that a propeller designed for use in water will behave differently when it encounters a compressible fluid.

Although many might just waive their hands about at me when I say this it important to consider the compressibility of fluids as materials when you apply the discipline of materials science to your engineering formulations.

It seems like (and I may be wrong) you dont want something designed to cut Through the wind, if youre trying to Catch it.

Dunno.

author: @jonbell3531
text: Dear Robert sir, I think you may have it on backwards. If so, it won't have been a fare test. Looking at the video the wind will be deflected by the front edge of the blade then be turbulently sucked back round as it passes causing deceleration and drag. If it was on the other way round then after the initial deflection it would be further accelerated by the curve of the blade. Yes/No?

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: No, it's mounted the correct way. You can see the camber on the blades.

replies.author: @jonbell3531
replies.text: @tlangdon12 someone else already questioned the direction, describing it as set up for thrust. Turned 180 the "camber" would be similar but deflecting the blade to cause further acceleration, at least in low wind speeds. The way shown in the video seems that the initial leading angle is steeper but becomes shallower - should it not be shallower on the leading edge becoming steeper to add further acceleration?

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: @jonbell3531 The blade in the video is quite flexible so it will untwist under load. It doesn’t matter if the load is from it being driven by a motor or by the wind.

author: @scotttovey
text:
The design is to reduce cavitation which is to reduce the formation of bubbles or air pockets as it produces thrust.

In reverse, the blade is not spinning to produce thrust and therefore will not produce cavitation and does not need to reduce cavitation. 
In reverse, thrust that already exists in the wind, is causing the blade to spin.
In reverse, the blade is the consumer rather than the producer.
The blade is reducing the amount of thrust through consumption.

I think that your tests show that just because a blade is more efficient in producing thrust in water, that does not necessarily equate to being more efficient in producing thrust in air, and especially does not make it more efficient in converting the thrust of wind, into useful energy.

It may also mean that, the claim of the design being more efficient in water is actually not true and just a marketing scheme.

author: @grunwode

author: @totherarf
text:
I think the reason for the popularity of the Toroidal Propeller is several fold .....

1. It started off in the marine industry where there are definite advantages due to lack of cavitation.
2. It made a Noise as a quiet prop for drones. Whilst this is less obvious the high spin rate and the formation of vortices from traditional blades causing noise the obverse is also true.
3. It is new and sexy, MIT have jumped on the bandwagon ( prostituting an organisation like that is beyond me .... but it is American) and everyone jumps on board .... and they can't be wrong because everyone else is doing the same! It reminds me of Tulips!

Interesting fact ...... In a plane driven by propellers most of the noise is generated not by the engine, but by the propeller! (At least in modern planes, Not sure about some of the WarBirds)

author: @jerrodlopes186
text:
I would be willing to bet that the pitch of the blades is just too steep for the application. Flatten it out a fair number of degrees and you will likely see a big difference. Also, there appears to be no chord line to this toroidal prop you were given. It looks on video like a flat ribbon that was twisted into shape. There needs to be an arc to its surface area in order to fully utilize the air pressure being directed at it. In this case you want an impeller rather than a propeller, so the inverse orientation is what is needed...I think 🤔.

*Not an EXPERT, just a guy with a lot of boating and aviation experience (propellers).

author: @robinvince616
text: Well done Robert! A resistive load is much more sensible than an LED which just clamps the output voltage to the LED array's natural forward voltage characteristic, allowing it to light even with a ridiculously small amount of current. But a fairer test still, would be to choose a load resistor that causes the output voltage to drop to half of its off-load value. Now do that with your two different designs of propeller under the same wind conditions, and compare the resultant powers produced.

author: @justtinkering6713
text: Did you know that different resistive loads produce different power? For a given rpm of the alternator there is one resistance that produces the most power. Voltage times current. At other resistances the voltage is highest and others the current is highest. But only one resistance that produces the most watts. My theory is that that ideal resistance is equal to the inductive reactance of the alternator stator at that rpm. Change the rpm/ frequency and the ideal load resistance changes also.

author: @newmonengineering
text: I think their application in water is the key. They are more efficient in liquid and prevent cavitation pitting which was the intention. They may work in air but they need some redesign to work efficiently in air. The current versions in water are very good but air and water are 2 different things. Although they obey some similar fluid dynamics there is no comparison to the drag and density of them. Maybe someone smarter could design one that works well with air but until then these should remain for boats in my view.

replies.text:
I understand that cavitation damage to props reduces efficiency, and is also is expensive to repair/replace.
I do not understand the relative economics of maintenance vs efficiency, but I can very much see that this design could save on both, so it must be a winner(?) :)

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: @pompeymonkey3271 Think if you never had to replace the propeller on a cargo ship, ferry, or large yacht.  Theoretically, no cavitation, no steam damage, no extraordinary stress or degradation could result in props you can use not for the lifetime of the powerplant but the lifetime of the *vessel* itself.

But they're harder to make, and I doubt you could make these as big as an equivalent normal blade.

replies.author: @pir869
replies.text:
yes water and air have the difference in that one is wet and the other is dry,but that is where the differences stop other than water is more viscous than air both are fluid mediums and react in the same manner,so if the toroidal blade can have less input power to get the same thrust or water movement throughput then the same can be said for a toroidal blade in air.

Reverse this and the same wind speed should create more torque for the same water or air throughput versus a straight fan blade.

Be aware that  stepper motors do not rotate freely like a normal dc motor/generator.And are not the device of choice for a generator or motor  application ,they are used for precision movement in cnc applications.

I'm not convinced that the test was done in the best way.

replies.author: @tlangdon12
replies.text: No, it won't generate more power if it was reversed. That way round, it would only produce power using the drag of the blades. As Robert had it held, it was generating power using the lift that the blades produce as the air moves over them. This movement of air is exactly the same relative movement that the blades would encounter if they were powered by a motor or engine.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
I agree with having a prejudice from the start. my prejudice is that an increased surface area will likly result in poorer performance.

 It is also  good to put our prejudices aside and make a proper comparison.  But I disagree that testing with a fixed resistance is a proper comparison, because  efficiency depends on tip speed ratio. And unfortunately, a fixed resistance does not allow one to vary the tip speed ratio. One simply need glance at a graph showing coefficient of performance as a function of tip speed ratio.

 I'm not saying this turbine will have any tip speed ratio that results in a greater efficiency, but one can't prove that with some radomly chosen fixed load.  

I Love what your doing on your channel but your test here, all by it self, is almost a useless comparison.😃

You are shockingly incurious. I'm disappointed in you and will unsubscribe and ignore your channel. It's really embarrassing for you to throw stones given the impractical bric a brac you usually go on about. Is it an ego thing or what?

Wings have, like, a zillion parameters, and wind turbine blades are built at the continuously improving limits of what the materials can support. A comparably efficient blade set that improved stiffness would already be important. No comment on how to ship a 150 m radius toroidal propeller blade, though.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
A traditional wind turbine with four  blades has a peak efficiency slightly less than than two blades. The presumption is increased surface area results in increased resistance  But the four blade peek efficency is at about half the the rate of rotation and the pitch needed to be changed.  

But because the wind remained the same, the same energy is in the wind,  and because the rate of rotation was halfed, the voltage from  the same generator was halfed. Rhetorically, how were the researches able to test and come up with roughly equlivent  peek efficency? Because they didn't use a fixed resistance. 

One may  look up "graph, tip speed ratio or TSR, and coefficient of performance"  and take note that for any given wind and turbine, the only way to vary the TSR is to vary the load resistance. 

I'm not sugesting this MIT inspired  blade will have a peak TSR  that is better than a traditional turbine. Mater of fact I would place money on a bet that it won't. But I am saying that a test with just one randomly chosen resistance is a near useless test on its own.  

And while I'm not critical of entering a comparison with a prejudice. I am critical of conclusions resulting from testing methods that are incapable of supporting the conclusion. One may as well just stick with their prejudice and not test because they think a higher surface area always means more drag and lower efficiency. 

In summary, To find the peak efficiency of any turbine and generator, you need to vary the load to vary the turbine's TSR that when graphed, results in an efficency  curve with an optimal efficiency at one load dependent TSR.

author: @tractorguy97

replies.author: @technosaurus3805
replies.text: When you use the equations for laminar flow but use the device in turbulent flow, the numbers won't add up. It's a difficult problem to solve in the real world... most US Navy submarine screws (propellers) are classified to protect the research that has gone into (almost) solving this problem in water, at different pressures and speeds. An enlisted submariner (usually a nuke mechanic) with dive quals has to cover it before going into drydock.
